2: People of Zarahemla] See Omni 1:12-19. When Mosiah I led the more righteous portion of the Nephites northward from the land of Lehi-Nephi (about 200 B.C.) he found on the west bank of the river Sidon a city inhabited by a people whose language he could not understand. They were ruled by a king named Zarahemla. When the two races developed the ability to communicate, it was discovered that the people of Zarahemla were the descendants of a colony which had been led by the Lord out of Jerusalem in the year when that city was destroyed by the king of Babylon (587 B.C.). After wandering in the wilderness they were brought across the ocean and landed in the Americas. In the years that followed, they migrated southward to the place where they were found by Mosiah. Because they had brought no scriptural or written records with them their language, society, and spiritual nature had all devolved or become corrupted. (Joseph Fielding Mc Conkie and Robert L. Millet, Doctrinal Commentary on the Book of Mormon, 4 vols., 2:, p.288)

The merger of the Nephites and Mulekites is recounted with very little explanation in the Book of Mormon and presents some anomalies. Since Zarahemla, leader of the Mulekites, was descended from Zedekiah, king of Judah (see Omni 1:14-15; Mosiah 25:2; Helaman 6:10; 8:21), we are perplexed by the fact that he acknowledged Mosiah as king over the new confederation. It may be that we are dealing with the age-old leadership conflict between the biblical tribes of Judah (represented by Zarahemla) and Joseph (represented by Mosiah). If the Book of Mormon story is truly analogous to the biblical, we should expect that some of the Mulekites objected to Nephite rule and occasionally rebelled against those in power. There is, in fact, evidence that the rebellious king-men and Nehors may have been Mulekites. (John A. Tvedtnes in Stephen D. Ricks and William J. Hamblin, eds., Warfare in the Book of Mormon, p.298)

The earliest manuscript spelling for the surviving son of king Zedekiah reads Muloch (in Mosiah 25:2 of the printer's manuscript). On the other hand, this name is spelled Mulek in Helaman 6 - 8 of the printer's manuscript. This alternative spelling is probably due to the nearby influence of 13 occurrences of the name of the city Mulek (consistently spelled as such in both manuscripts, from Alma 51 through Helaman 5). Note that the spelling Muloch suggests an ominous connection with the god Molech /Moloch (to which children in Israel were sacrificed prior to the Babylonian captivity?see 1 Kings 11:7 - 8, 2 Kings 23:10, and Acts 7:43). (Royal Skousen's Analysis of Textual Variants of the Book of Mormon)

Mulek] Mulek was the son of Zedekiah, king of Judea, who was preserved when the rest of his brothers were slain by the king of Babylon (see 2 Kings 25:7). It is quite probable that because of his lineage Mulek was appointed the king of the wandering colony of which he was a part. Through this colony the blood of Judah was destined to be mixed with the blood of Joseph in the Americas. (Joseph Fielding Mc Conkie and Robert L. Millet, Doctrinal Commentary on the Book of Mormon, 4 vols., 2:, p.288)
4: 4-11. History gives us cause to both lament and rejoice. It is as the voice of a wise parent. In it we find meaning and direction. Through it we find courage and purpose. By giving heed to the lessons of the past we can be warned and spared, or by ignoring those lessons we create the need for having them retaught with painful clarity. The value of accurate records, the importance of both sacred and secular history, can hardly be overstated. (Joseph Fielding Mc Conkie and Robert L. Millet, Doctrinal Commentary on the Book of Mormon, 4 vols., 2:, p.289)
7: Mosiah's intent was not to entertain his listeners. His purpose in reading the histories of Zeniff and Alma was to humble his own followers and to help them profit from the mistakes of others. Thus, by use of the recorded materials, he took his people from the depths of sorrow to the peak of concern for the welfare of their enemies. And, whether he intended to do so or not, he surely must have reinforced in the minds of his listeners many of the reasons for record-keeping, including those already cited, which are: o The preservation of language. o The preservation of the words of the prophets. o The preservation of lineage and priesthood rights. o The preservation of faith-promoting experiences. o The retention of Church members. o The preservation of personal memories. o The welding of the readers (children) to the writers (parents). These reasons and others that could be listed should convince us of the need to plant the "pecan tree" practice of personal and family histories. It should be our desire to "keep a proper and faithful record" of our lives (see D&C 128:9). In the end, we will have preserved for ourselves and for future generations a handful of "pennies" of worth. (Carlos E. Asay, Family Pecan Trees: Planting a Legacy of Faith at Home, p.118-119)
10: goodness (Heb. tuwb) to be good, be pleasing, be joyful, be beneficial
11: Both the righteous and the wicked suffer anguish of soul, meaning excruciating distress and extreme pain of body and mind. The righteous suffer anguish in this life because of the sins and rebellion of their brethren. (1 Ne. 17:47; 2 Ne. 26:7; Mosiah 25:11; Alma 8:14; Morm. 6:16.) Christ himself suffered until blood came from every pore, so great was "his anguish for the wickedness and the abominations of his people." (Mosiah 3:7.) (Bruce R. Mc Conkie, Mormon Doctrine, 2d ed., p.38)

Take special note of the repeated reference to the welfare of souls. This implies much more than just food, clothing, and shelter for temporal needs. (Robert D. Hales, in Conference Report, Apr. 1986, 36; or Ensign, May 1986, 29)
12: Children Who Disinherit Their Parents by S. Kent Brown Religious Studies Center Newsletter~May 1999 A Book of Mormon passage that speaks of children effectively disinheriting their fathers invites comment. In From Jerusalem to Zarahemla, I have mentioned this matter only incidentally, in the chapter "Marriage and Treaty in the Book of Mormon: The Case of the Abducted Lamanite Daughters" (108 n. 8). The fathers were former priests of King Noah who had abandoned their families during a Lamanite invasion (Mosiah 19:10-12,18-21). Their leader was a man named Amulon. Two years after abandoning their families, these men kidnapped Lamanite women to be their wives, thus precipitating another Lamanite invasion (Mosiah 20:1-7). The passage in question reads: "The children of Amulon and his brethren ... were displeased with the conduct of their fathers, and they would no longer be called by the names of their fathers, therefore they took upon themselves the name of Nephi, that they might be called the children of Nephi" (Mosiah 25: 12). Two points draw our attention. First, I am not aware of any passage in the annals of ancient law that allowed an abandoned child effectively to divorce one parent or the other. Second, I am not aware of any ancient society that permitted a child to choose the person to whom he or she would be linked. Yet the passage evidently portrays both circumstances. It is possible, of course, that there was no legal provision in Nephite law that would allow these children to act as they did. If this observation is true, then authorities may have allowed their 3 actions as symbolic gestures. Such gestures, however, would not have solved certain questions tied to inheriting property from the children's biological fathers nor to potential responsibility for inherited debts. But Mormon did not raise these matters in his record since they were apparently not germane to his purposes. What had caught his attention instead was the children's unusual and strong response to the actions of their fathers. The Hammurapi Code, compiled in the eighteenth century B.C., legislated important standards that may beam light on the children's actions. According to laws 168-69, if a father wanted to disinherit a son (the reverse of the Book of Mormon case), there had to be evidence of a "grave offense." Additionally, for the first offense, the judges of a case between a father and a son were to "pardon" the son. It is only "if he should commit a grave offense a second time" that the father was allowed to "disinherit his son." The case of the Nephite children fits this requirement because their fathers had first abandoned their wives and children and then, in a separate, second offense, kidnapped the Lamanite women. Other legislation in the Hammurapi Code may connect to the Book of Mormon situation. According to

laws 192-93, which deal specifically with adopted children, a child's attempt to reject adoptive parents was not allowed and, in fact, carried severe penalties. Because the statute stood in Hammurapi's Code, it clearly hints that adopted children had tried such actions. In this light, to override such a standard would presumably require that a parent, adoptive or natural, commit extraordinary crimes. While it cannot be shown that this kind of standard operated among Nephites, the extreme situation that led to the children's actions would possibly, under the code of Hammurapi, have allowed their extraordinary procedure. (Citations are from Martha 1. Roth, Law Collections from Mesopotamia and Asia Minor, Writings from the Ancient World, Society of Biblical Literature, vol. 6 [Atlanta: Scholars Press, 1995], 113, 120.) However, although both Nephite and later Jewish law descended from Old Testament law, one must not step into the trap of seeing Nephite law through the provisions of Jewish law. For example, statutes in Jewish law address the legal status of women who lose husbands, determining the woman's inheritance rights and her right to remarry (e.g., Mishnah, Yebamoth 10.1-5; 15.1-16.7; Shebuoth 7.7). But we cannot assume that such statutes would mirror those that developed among the Nephites and would therefore illuminate the situation of the children's abandoned mothers. The social, religious, and military situations that deprived women of husbands in the two societies were very different through the centuries and would have given rise to different pressures as laws developed.

The children of Amulon] See Mosiah 20:3-5. Amulon was one of King Noah's priests who undoubtedly took an active part in the martyrdom of Abinadi. When Noah was burned to death by his enraged subjects, Amulon was among those who fled into the wilderness. Having abandoned their wives and children for fear of their own safety, these ignoble priests then kidnapped some Lamanite maidens, whom they took with them in their wilderness wanderings. The Lamanites, supposing that their daughters had been taken by those of the city of Nephi, then attacked the Nephites, which resulted in the loss of many lives (see Mosiah 20). Later these priests made an alliance with the Lamanites and were made rulers over Alma and those of the city of Helam. Their brutality and injustice caused Alma and his people to implore the Lord to deliver them. Ashamed of the actions of their forbears, the posterity of

“In the past twenty years, as homes and families have struggled to stay intact, sociological studies reveal this alarming fact: much of the crime and many of the behavioral disorders in the United States come from homes where the father has abandoned the children. In many societies the world over, child poverty, crime, drug abuse, and family decay can be traced to conditions where the father gives no male nurturing….We need to honor the position of the father as the primary provider for physical and spiritual support. I state this with no reluctance because the Lord has revealed that this obligation is placed upon husbands….(DC 83:2,4; 84:99; 29:48).” (James E. Faust. Ensign, May 1993, pp. 35-36)
13: The citizenry of Mosiah's kingdom consisted of a union of Nephites and Mulekites. Though the Mulekites were more numerous (verse 2), they chose to call themselves Nephites. (Joseph Fielding Mc Conkie and Robert L. Millet, Doctrinal Commentary on the Book of Mormon, 4 vols., 2:, p.289)
15: 15-17. Though we are told that Alma's message was faith and repentance, we are also told that he taught "many things." Majestic mountains are but a home for all manner of foliage and animal life. Even so, the towering doctrines of faith and repentance provide a terrain natural and conducive to the teaching of all the truths of salvation. (Joseph Fielding Mc Conkie and Robert L. Millet, Doctrinal Commentary on the Book of Mormon, 4 vols., 2:, p.290)
16: remember (Heb. zakar) to make a memorial, make remembrance
18: As many as he did baptize did belong to the church] Those worthily baptized obtain, through this ordinance, membership in the Church and thus citizenship in both the earthly and heavenly kingdoms of God. (Joseph Fielding Mc Conkie and Robert L. Millet, Doctrinal Commentary on the Book of Mormon, 4 vols., 2:, p.291)
19: Gave him power to ordain priests and teachers] Mosiah obviously held the keys of the kingdom. That is, the right of presidency rested with him, and Alma could take no action in organizing various bodies of the church except under his direction. (Joseph Fielding Mc Conkie and Robert L. Millet, Doctrinal Commentary on the Book of Mormon, 4 vols., 2:, p.291)
21: Different bodies, being called churches] In all gospel dispensations those who have entered into the waters of baptism have been commanded to meet together often "to fast and to pray, and to speak one with another concerning the welfare of their souls" (Moroni 6:5). (Joseph Fielding Mc Conkie and Robert L. Millet, Doctrinal Commentary on the Book of Mormon, 4 vols., 2:, p.291)
22: The church of God is one church. (Mosiah 25:22.) However, because its members are numerous and live in geographically diverse places, it is necessary to establish local congregations for the teaching and governance of the people. Each of the Nephite congregations was referred to as a church in Mosiah 25:21, but they were all being taught the word of God from the mouth of Alma, through the priests and teachers appointed to administer the affairs of the church in their area. Just as today, when the members of the Church of Jesus Christ are taught the word of God from the mouth of the prophet through the bishop and stake president appointed to administer the affairs of the church in their area, these people received a single message. In their case, as is true today, nothing was preached except repentance and faith in God. And in their case, as is true today, "the Lord did pour out his Spirit upon them, and they were blessed, and prospered in the land." (Mosiah 25:24.) (Kay P. Edwards in Kent P. Jackson, ed., Studies in Scripture, Vol. 7: 1 Nephi to Alma 29, p.272)

“’The Church is the same wherever you go!’ I grew up hearing this from returning vacationers and from missionaries reporting their missions. And I understood what they meant. They didn't mean that the same hymns were sung elsewhere- though they were- or that everyone used the same lesson manuals, or that everyone thought the same. They meant the gospel felt the same wherever they went. This resulted from the presence of the Holy Spirit, which is the universal way we recognize the things of God. It's the same when you learn something new or see something from a new slant, and suddenly it all makes sense. You know it's right, because it feels right and is consistent with other glimpses of truth that you've had.” (Lauri Hlavaty in Monte S. Nyman and Charles D. Tate, Jr., eds., Jacob through Words of Mormon: To Learn with Joy, p.105)

Being many churches they were all one church] As there was a unity of doctrine among all the churches in Zarahemla, so there has been a unity of doctrine among all the churches in all times and in all dispensations. It is not to be supposed that the Church did not previously exist in Zarahemla but rather that because of the great increase in its population it was now necessary to divide the Church into various congregations. In modern terms we would think in terms of making ward and stake divisions. (Joseph Fielding Mc Conkie and Robert L. Millet, Doctrinal Commentary on the Book of Mormon, 4 vols., 2:, p.291)
